nativeRange.habitat: Found on muddy and sandy bottoms of inshore bays, lagoons, and sandy-rubble flats, typically at depths of 3–25 m. This species digs and uses burrows for refuge.

diet.notes: Feeds by sifting mouthfuls of sand to extract small invertebrates, organic matter, and microorganisms from the substrate. In aquaria, has been reported to accept sinking pellets, mysis shrimp, and finely chopped frozen foods. A live sand bed with a healthy microfauna community is generally advised to supplement its natural sifting behavior. Continuous sand-sifting is typically observed throughout the day.

imageCandidates.notes: Photo credit: Brett, O.J. / www.tropicalfavourites.com. Thumbnail; full-resolution image access may require navigating FishBase directly.

summary: The Orangemarked Goby is a small sand-sifting species from the Western Central Pacific, found in shallow bays and lagoons from the Philippines to New Caledonia. Keeping one well generally requires a deep sand bed and some patience with feeding.
careNotes:
The Orangemarked Goby — also traded as the Orange-striped Goby or Pink-lined Goby (Amblygobius decussatus) — is a sand-sifting species native to inshore bays and lagoons of the Western Central Pacific, ranging from the Philippines to New Caledonia and throughout Micronesia.

A sand substrate of at least 5–10 cm depth is widely considered important, as this species is documented using burrows for refuge and spends much of the day sifting the sand bed in search of food. A tank footprint of at least 60 cm length is generally advised to provide adequate foraging territory, and a minimum volume of 100 L is recommended to give the fish sufficient sand area and stable water quality.

The Orangemarked Goby tends to be peaceful toward most tank mates but may be territorial with conspecifics and similar-looking sand-sifting gobies. Keeping a mated pair is possible in larger tanks, but two males in close quarters may show aggression toward one another. The species is generally considered reef-safe, as it does not bother corals.

Feeding may require some patience at first. While many individuals gradually accept prepared and frozen foods, consistent target feeding with sinking pellets, mysis shrimp, or finely chopped frozen foods is often needed to ensure adequate nutrition beyond what the sand bed provides. A live sand bed with copepods, amphipods, and small worms is generally considered beneficial for the long-term health of this species.

Like most gobies, the Orangemarked Goby carries a moderate escape risk, and a tight-fitting lid or cover is generally advised, particularly for tanks with low rockwork walls.
breedingNotes: No established captive-breeding protocol is widely documented for this species in the aquarium hobby. As with many sand-dwelling gobies, breeding behavior in captivity has occasionally been reported in pairs, but details are sparse in available sources.

Confirmed scientific name, max size (9.5 cm SL), temperature range (22–30°C, preferred 26.3–29°C), native range (Western Central Pacific: Philippines to New Caledonia, Yaeyama Islands, Great Barrier Reef, Micronesia), habitat (marine/brackish; muddy bottoms of inshore bays and lagoons, 3–25 m), diet (sand-sifter, small invertebrates and organic matter), and taxonomy (Gobiidae / Gobiiformes). fish.breedingNotes: No established captive-breeding protocol is widely documented for this species. Occasional reports of spawning in captive pairs exist but detailed protocols are not available in major hobby sources.
